content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916} 322-0886 
Mathew G. Jacobs 
General Counsel 
Marte Castaiios 
Senior Staff Attorney 
Legal Office 
P.O. Box 942707 
Sacramento, CA 94229-2707 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-14-2ll(a) 
Dear Mr. Jacobs and Mr. Castaiios: 
January 27, 2014 
This letter responds to your request for follow up advice regarding gift provi!lions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act").i You specifically asked for clarification of the following 
advice in.our letter Jacobs Advice Letter, No. A-14-211: 
"/. Ill cases where Ca/PERS pays for employees to attend a conference, are 
meals and refreshments available to all such attendees at the co11fere11ce exempt 
from gift reporti11g? Does the fact that meals a11d refresh111e11ts are listed with a 
11omillal sponsor 011 the conference agenda change the co11c/usio11? 
"In casei. where Cal PERS purchases an admission to a conference, CalPERS has 
purchased the access to the conference and everything included in the admission 
fee. Similar to where Cal PERS enters into a contract to purchase goods and 
training for its employees. CalPERS will have purchased these benefite and can 
use them in any way it deems fit. Generally, reimbursement for expenses or per 
diem received from a state, local, or federal government agency are exempt from 
both the gift and income definitions in the Act. (Section 82030(b)(2).)'" 
1 The Poltw:al Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutor)' 
rcfcrcm:es arc to the Go, ernment Code. unles<; otherwise indicated. The regulations or the Fair Political Practices 
Commissmn arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
n:gulator} reference.!. arc to Tith; 2. Di, 1sion 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-14-211 (a) 
Page No. 2 
On January 26, 2015, you asked for clarification as to under what circumstances 
Cal PERS is considered to have "paid for" employees to attend a conference. 
The reason you ask for this clarification was that in the facts of your original request, you 
noted that the rates charged to CalPERS to attend these conferences vary. 
"CaIPERS staff regularly attends conferences to remain informed about 
developments in benefit and investment matters. The rates charged to attend these 
conferences vary from several thousand dollars to free admission. As a 
government agency and the administrator of a public pension plan, CaIPERS staff 
is often afforded the opportunity to attend conferences at a reduced rate (e.g., 
CaIPERS is often offered an Institutional Investor' or 'Plan Sponsor" rate.) At 
other times, it is offered a "government" rate." 
For example, in the case you asked about "CaIPERS is eligible to utilize the 
Government/Regulator rate ($0), the non-member Investor rate ($250), or the Standard rate 
($2, 195)." 
We did not address the rate structure in our response to you. 
As we discussed by telephone, our letter should have made clear that the same co11cl11sio11 
would applv wizen Ca/PERs "acquires" the conference admi.fsion at the full rate or a discounted 
or free govemment rate and then permits its emplovees to use the admission. The advice was not 
based on the cost of admission to Cal PERS, but rather on the relationship between CalPERS and 
its employees and that fact that government salary and benefits provided to an employee are 
generally not reportable. 
Thus, we amend the first conclusion in our letter to read: 
1. In the case where CalPERS acquires the admission (whether at the full rate or 
discounted or free government rate) and the admission includes meals for the attending 
employees, no gift occurs to the employees. The fact that CalPERS acquires these benefits of 
admission to the conference and makes them available to attending employees is a nonreportable 
event. This is true whether a "sponsor" for the food is listed or not. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660 . 
. Wallace 
JWW:jgl

sections.question: Under what circumstances is CalPERS considered to have "paid for" employees to attend a conference, particularly given that rates charged to CalPERS vary from several thousand dollars to free admission, and CalPERS is often afforded reduced rates (e.g., Institutional Investor, Plan Sponsor, or government rates)?
sections.conclusion: In the case where CalPERS acquires the admission (whether at the full rate or discounted or free government rate) and the admission includes meals for the attending employees, no gift occurs to the employees. The fact that CalPERS acquires these benefits of admission to the conference and makes them available to attending employees is a nonreportable event. This is true whether a "sponsor" for the food is listed or not.
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor seeks clarification on when CalPERS is deemed to have paid for employee conference attendance, given that conference admission rates vary significantly and CalPERS often receives discounted or free government rates.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC clarifies that CalPERS employees receive no reportable gifts when CalPERS acquires conference admission at any rate (full, discounted, or free) and makes meals and refreshments available to attendees, regardless of whether a sponsor is listed.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a follow-up letter amending conclusions from a prior advice letter (A-14-211). The document includes both the original question and amended conclusion. Date on letter shows January 27, 2014, but text references January 26, 2015 follow-up discussion; this appears to be a 2015 letter with 2014 file number designation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Under what circumstances is CalPERS considered to have "paid for" employees to attend a conference, particularly given that rates charged to CalPERS vary from several thousand dollars to free admission, and CalPERS is often afforded reduced rates (e.g., Institutional Investor, Plan Sponsor, or government rates)?

CONCLUSION: In the case where CalPERS acquires the admission (whether at the full rate or discounted or free government rate) and the admission includes meals for the attending employees, no gift occurs to the employees. The fact that CalPERS acquires these benefits of admission to the conference and makes them available to attending employees is a nonreportable event. This is true whether a "sponsor" for the food is listed or not.

embedding.summary: This is a follow-up advice letter clarifying the FPPC's previous guidance (A-14-211) regarding gift reporting for meals provided at conferences paid for by CalPERS. The FPPC amends its conclusion to clarify that the same non-reportable gift analysis applies whether CalPERS acquires conference admission at full or discounted/free government rates.
